title: Long Movie Trailer Script Candidates
summary: Long poetic trailer scripts for turning product problems into protagonist stories before later video generation.
selectionNote: These are script assets first. They are written as rhymed heroic-couplet style voiceovers with iambic-pentameter-ish flow, so they can become longer ElevenLabs reads now and video trailers later.

items.angle: Gary survives the great leak by turning memory into confirmed job recall
items.voiceDirection: Lily for warm storybook trailer, or Yorkshire David for comic tradesperson trailer.
items.claimPosture: comic fiction built on JobDone Product Truth
items.title: JobDone - Gary And The Great Leak
items.scriptText:
In dawn's cold hush, when pipes began to cry,
brave Gary raised his wrench against the sky.

The street below lay shining, wide, and wet;
the tenants watched with faces full of fret.

He found the valve, he stemmed the silver flood,
then patched the joint with tape and honest mud.

The boiler sighed, the cupboard ceased to rain,
and all the flats were peaceful once again.

They cheered his name from stair and kitchen door;
no plumber there had seemed so wise before.

But time, that thief, came creeping through the lane,
and brought the selfsame leak to life again.

Poor Gary stared where once his triumph stood;
he knew he fixed it, but not how he could.

Was that the bend behind the second wall?
Was Mrs Green the tenant he should call?

Which part had split? Which washer had he used?
Which stopcock stuck? Which note had he refused?

His photos slept in chats from long ago;
his voice notes mumbled things he could not know.

His memory, once mighty, brave, and bright,
went clanking down the drain that rainy night.

Then, from the phone that shivered in his hand,
a hum arose, as calm as solid land.

JobDone appeared: not magic, smoke, or flame,
but one clear place to keep the job by name.

He spoke the facts while boots were still on floor;
what failed, what changed, who owned the cellar door.

He checked the words, confirmed the proper place,
and sealed the entry in its rightful space.

The Timeline held what Gary could not hold;
the Contact, Location, date, and story told.

Next time the leak returned with wicked grin,
our Gary tapped Recall, and looked within.

The source came back, not guesswork in disguise:
the fitting size, the note, the warning signs.

He walked in sure, with history in his hand,
and made the water bend to his command.

No invoice spell, no chatbot dressed as law;
just grounded memory for the jobs he saw.

Capture the work, confirm the truth you know,
then ask the past which way the pipes should go.

So Gary smiled, his boots no longer blue;
he fixed the leak, and knew what he must do.

JobDone remembers what the work has shown.
Go to jobdone.continuumkit.org.
items.productionNotes: Use storm drain, pipe hiss, heroic orchestral rise, phone confirmation chime, and a final practical stamp. This can be read as a 2 to 2.5 minute trailer with long pauses and scene beats.
items.meterNotes: Rhymed couplets with approximate iambic pentameter. Keep performance poetic and clear rather than forcing exact scansion.

items.audience: field workers, sailors, land workers, busy-hands people
items.angle: Mara cannot use a screen while the real world keeps moving
items.voiceDirection: Lily only. Warm, capable, slightly cinematic.
items.title: Field Relay - Mara And The Busy Hands
items.scriptText:
Where signal fades and weather writes the day,
young Mara worked where screens got in the way.

Her gloves were wet, her notebook blown and torn;
her list was gone before the second horn.

The gate was loose, the pump had coughed at three,
the cracked old fitting hid behind a tree.

A sailor called, a farmer waved her near,
a warning note dissolved before her ear.

She had two hands, but five small fires to tend;
she had one phone, but could not stop and bend.

The world kept asking, now, before, and next;
her thumbs were cold, her tired eyes were vexed.

At dusk she found, beneath a dripping coat,
the ghost of every half-remembered note.

What field was that? Which tool was left behind?
Which job belonged to which half-busy mind?

Then Field Relay came softly through the rain,
a field computer fitted for the plain.

Not always hearing, not a hidden spy;
just ready when a busy voice says why.

She spoke the note while holding fast the gate;
she logged the place before the thought was late.

She called the checklist, marked the broken part,
and sent the job toward her waiting chart.

A tricorder, but built for mud and rope;
a command line dressed in weatherproof hope.

Bring your own agent, teach your own command;
build tools that fit the work beneath your hand.

The pump was fixed, the field report was clear;
the sailor found the kit she left last year.

The phone became no tyrant in her palm,
but one small relay, useful, quick, and calm.

For eyes on work and hands that cannot pause,
for muddy boots and practical causes:

Field Relay waits where ordinary screens
cannot keep up with what the fieldwork means.

Part of the Continuum family.
Go to fieldrelay.continuumkit.org.
items.productionNotes: Use rain, gate latch, rope, distant radio, boot steps, and restrained heroic rise. Avoid claiming always-on capture or a finished public product.
items.meterNotes: Rhymed couplets with approximate iambic-pentameter cadence.

items.angle: Ava stops waiting for a technical cofounder and learns to wrangle agents
items.title: AI Developer School - Ava Learns The New Language
items.scriptText:
Ava had plans that filled a bedroom wall;
bright cards and arrows, stacked above them all.

She knew the buyer, pain, and price by heart,
yet every build attempt fell apart.

The code looked strange, the tools changed every day;
the demos died in tabs along the way.

She asked a model, watched it nod and spin,
then lost the thread before the work began.

The dream was clear; the method would not land.
She needed more than wishes in her hand.

Then one line struck like morning through the glass:
the new programming language is human language.

Not vague desire, not prompts that beg and plead;
but sharp intent, good taste, and tested need.

She learned to name the truth before the sell,
to brief the agent, then inspect it well.

She made small loops where feedback could reply,
and killed weak claims before they learned to fly.

She wrote the advert, built the rough first screen,
then asked what proof would make the meaning clean.

No magic throne, no genius in a jar;
just better steering by a clearer star.

The agents worked when Ava set the frame;
the product found a shape, a price, a name.

Her first rough demo limped, but it was real;
her first crude advert showed what people feel.

She shipped before the energy was gone,
then shaped the next attempt from what was shown.

For founders lost between idea and code,
for clever minds that need a shorter road:

Learn product truth, learn prompts that hold their spine;
learn when to trust, and when to draw the line.

Wrangle the agents. Build the business.
Part of the Continuum family.
Go to school.continuumkit.org.
items.productionNotes: Use typing, whiteboard markers, small synth pulse, and rising educational trailer music. Good candidate for a long flagship read.
items.meterNotes: Rhymed couplets with poetic cadence. The key line intentionally breaks meter slightly for memorability.

items.audience: neurodiverse builders, idea-heavy people, AI-heavy solo operators
items.angle: Nora's ideas arrive faster than systems can hold them
items.voiceDirection: Lily. Kind, precise, not medical, not patronising.
items.title: Continuum - Nora Finds The Thread
items.scriptText:
Nora could see ten bridges in one spark;
then lose the map by morning in the dark.

Her mind made storms where other minds made lists;
bright links appeared, then vanished into mist.

A note began; another stole the page.
A thought grew wings before it found a cage.

The inbox filled with fragments, names, and starts;
her greatest plans were scattered into parts.

They called it chaos. She called it a day.
The thread was there, then always slipped away.

She did not need a louder self-help bell,
or one more box where good intentions fell.

She needed paths from capture back to why,
a way to tell the work: you did not die.

Then Continuum came quiet, spare, and plain;
not one more brain, but rails to find the train.

A private loop, made public-safe with care,
where agents find the context waiting there.

The capture held the spark that crossed her mind;
the status showed what mattered, not the grind.

Decisions sat where future work could see;
next actions stopped pretending to be free.

The storm still came; no system made it small.
But now the thread could answer when she'd call.

No finished kit in one triumphant click;
real workflows grow from what is true and thick.

For people rich in thought and poor in shelves,
who need a way to work with future selves:

Capture the thread. Recover the context.
Keep moving when the old trail is lost.

Continuum Kit.
Go to continuumkit.org.
items.productionNotes: Use sparse piano, soft ticks, thread/loom motif, and a warm resolved final note. Keep product status honest.
items.meterNotes: Rhymed couplets with approximate iambic-pentameter cadence; final tag deliberately becomes spoken slogan.

items.angle: Barnaby turns chaotic boatyard nonsense into a filmable challenge
items.voiceDirection: Lily deadpan over pirate/hornpipe music, or later a comic host voice.
items.title: Jury Rigged - Barnaby And The Questionable Boat
items.scriptText:
Barnaby dreamed of glory on the tide;
his crew brought tape, a bucket, and their pride.

The task was clear: cross water, ring the bell.
The plan, however, did not age so well.

One team found rope beneath a picnic chair;
one swapped a sail for half a garden stair.

The clever parts were hidden in plain sight;
the rules were fair, the methods less polite.

A head start waited where the puzzles lay;
a pulley prize could change the final day.

But chaos hates a camera with a plan;
it eats the shot before the shot began.

Who has the score? Which team may take the mast?
What safety line was checked before they passed?

Then Jury Rigged appeared beside the quay,
a concept built for making mayhem see.

It shaped the game before the shouting rose:
the run sheet, scoring, limits, shots, and close.

It marked the beats a later edit needs,
the hidden tools, the wins, the almost-deeds.

No promise here of magic finished shows;
just planned disorder, tied with better bows.

The final race began with fiddle fire;
the bucket sank, the staircase drifted higher.

Barnaby laughed, then found the winning clue:
the boring checklist saved the daft canoe.

The bell was rung, the splash was seen again;
the story held the shape inside the rain.

For creators chasing glorious mistakes,
for games where every plank and pulley shakes:

Design the puzzle. Capture the best moments.
Make the mayhem watchable.

Jury Rigged.
Part of the Continuum family.
Go to juryrigged.continuumkit.org.
items.productionNotes: Use hornpipe theme, flag snap, rope creak, cheering, comic splash, and bold trailer drums. Must remain 'concept', not shipped software.
items.meterNotes: Rhymed couplets with comic trailer cadence; some slogans at end break meter on purpose.

items.angle: Sam has product notes but no advert people can remember
items.title: AdLib - Sam And The Silent Product
items.productionNotes: Use paper, typing, waveform, short room-tone bed, and a warm end-card hit. This is a strong dogfood trailer.
items.meterNotes: Rhymed couplets with approximate iambic-pentameter cadence.

items.audience: water-sports video viewers and expedition followers
items.angle: Tom turns a chaotic downwind run into a watchable technical sports story
items.voiceDirection: Use sparingly. This may work better as narration plus stings, not pure advert.
items.title: Downwind - Tom And The Long Run
items.productionNotes: Probably generate as segmented narration plus video stings, not one long read. Use wave wipes, HUD beeps, glints, crashes, and sparse heroic music.
items.meterNotes: Rhymed couplets with sports-trailer cadence; designed to be cut around footage.
